summary:
Koxinga (Zheng Chenggong, 1624–1662 AD) was a Chinese-Japanese military commander and Ming loyalist who expelled the [[event:3305|Dutch from Taiwan]] (1662), established a Ming resistance state on the island, and became one of the most celebrated figures in both Chinese and Taiwanese historical memory. Born to a Chinese father (the merchant-pirate Zheng Zhilong) and a Japanese mother in Hirado, Japan, Koxinga inherited control of his father's maritime empire that controlled trade along the Chinese coast, and became the most formidable military opponent to the [[event:2416|Manchu Qing conquest of China]]. After the fall of the last Ming court in the south (1661), Koxinga led a fleet of 900 ships and 25,000 men across the Taiwan Strait, lay siege to the Dutch Fort Zeelandia for nine months, and forced its surrender in February 1662 — dying himself of illness four months later.

Koxinga's regime in Taiwan (under his son and grandson) was the last significant Ming resistance bastion, holding out against Qing authority until 1683, when a Qing fleet finally conquered the island. His legacy is complex and contested: in mainland China he is honored as a national hero who resisted foreign (Dutch) colonialism; in Taiwan he is celebrated as the founder of the first Chinese administration of the island; in Japan his story inspired the kabuki play 'The Battles of Coxinga' (1715) by Chikamatsu Monzaemon, one of the most popular theatrical works of the Edo period. Koxinga's expulsion of the Dutch and establishment of Chinese governance set the precedent for Taiwan's subsequent Chinese identity, while his anti-Qing resistance became symbolic in the nationalist politics of the 19th and 20th centuries.
